drafts.text:
Ofgem’s price cap drops to £1,641 for Apr to Jun 2026. (https://www.ofgem.gov.uk/information-consumers/energy-advice-households/energy-price-cap-explained)

That’s down 6.6% from £1,758 in Jan to Mar. (https://www.ofgem.gov.uk/information-consumers/energy-advice-households/energy-price-cap-explained)

DESNZ should now publish a plain plan for cutting standing charges, not just unit rates.

Blueprint is tracking this. https://britblueprint.com

CONTEXT: The UK borrowed £14.3bn in February 2026. That’s the second highest February on record. (https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/governmentpublicsectorandtaxes/publicsectorfinance/bulletins/publicsectorfinances/february2026)

RESULT: Borrowing was £2.2bn higher than February 2025, and £6.9bn above the OBR forecast. (https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/governmentpublicsectorandtaxes/publicsectorfinance/bulletins/publicsectorfinances/february2026)

CONSEQUENCES: Debt is 93.1% of GDP. That’s a 1960s-level burden sitting on every future Budget. (https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/governmentpublicsectorandtaxes/publicsectorfinance/bulletins/publicsectorfinances/february2026)

QUESTION: If debt is stuck near wartime-era highs, where is the delivery plan that makes the state cheaper to run? https://britblueprint.com

England has delivered an estimated 191,300 net additional homes since 1 April 2025. (https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/housing-supply-indicators-of-new-supply-england-october-to-december-2025/housing-supply-indicators-of-new-supply-england-october-to-december-2025)

Since the start of Parliament (9 July 2024), the estimate is 342,100. (https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/housing-supply-indicators-of-new-supply-england-october-to-december-2025/housing-supply-indicators-of-new-supply-england-october-to-december-2025)

The Housing Secretary should publish a monthly pipeline for sites blocked in planning, with named local accountability.

Blueprint is tracking this. https://britblueprint.com

The Lords voted 217 to 113 to strip ministers’ power to direct DC pension investments. (https://www.international-adviser.com/house-of-lords-votes-to-scrap-government-power-to-mandate-where-pension-schemes-invest/)

The fight is about a backstop power tied to the Mansion House Accord. (https://www.pensionsage.com/pa/Lords-vote-to-remove-govt-s-mandation-power-from-PSB.php)

The pensions minister, Torsten Bell, should now publish a voluntary plan with measurable timelines instead of reaching for compulsion.

Blueprint is tracking this. https://britblueprint.com

#100DayReform

I’ve spent enough time around capital markets to know one rule holds. Trust is an asset. Once you politicise it, you don’t get it back quickly.

This week the Lords voted 217 to 113 to remove ministers’ reserve power to direct where DC pension schemes invest. (https://www.international-adviser.com/house-of-lords-votes-to-scrap-government-power-to-mandate-where-pension-schemes-invest/)

If the UK wants more patient capital, the answer is better investable projects and better governance, not a statute-book threat over trustees.

This is exactly why I built @BritBlueprint. Inaction has a price tag. https://britblueprint.com

In markets, we’d call this a bad carry trade. You borrow at scale, then hope growth bails you out.

The ONS says the UK borrowed £14.3bn in February 2026 and debt sits at 93.1% of GDP. (https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/governmentpublicsectorandtaxes/publicsectorfinance/bulletins/publicsectorfinances/february2026)

That debt load narrows every future choice. It pushes governments towards short-term fixes and away from long-term reform.

I’m building @BritBlueprint to make delivery measurable. https://britblueprint.com

UK debt is no longer a background statistic. It is a delivery constraint.

The Office for National Statistics reported that the UK borrowed £14.3bn in February 2026. That was £2.2bn higher than the same month last year, and the second highest February since records began in 1993. The ONS also put public sector net debt at 93.1% of GDP at the end of February. (https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/governmentpublicsectorandtaxes/publicsectorfinance/bulletins/publicsectorfinances/february2026)

The gap is not a lack of plans. It is a lack of repeatable delivery. Borrowing keeps arriving in ugly monthly spikes because core systems still run hot: broken procurement, slow project approvals, and public bodies that can’t shift spend when demand changes. We see the same pattern across departments. Ministers announce targets. Budgets get allocated. Then the state can’t ship the work on time.

Fixing this does not require a new mega-spending promise. It requires named accountability for the cost drivers that sit behind monthly borrowing. Start with a published list of the 50 most expensive central government programmes by whole-life cost, each with a single senior responsible owner, a delivery scorecard, and a timetable to reset scope where it is slipping.

#UKPolicy #PublicFinance #BritBlueprint

I’ve run risk in markets where a single basis point move can wipe out months of work. Countries have the same problem. Small drifts compound into big constraints.

Here are the UK data hooks that stood out today.

1) Public finances
The ONS reported public sector borrowing of £14.3bn in February 2026. That was £2.2bn higher than February 2025, and £6.9bn above the OBR forecast. Debt is now 93.1% of GDP. (https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/governmentpublicsectorandtaxes/publicsectorfinance/bulletins/publicsectorfinances/february2026)

2) Household energy bills
Ofgem set the energy price cap for April to June 2026 at £1,641 for a typical dual-fuel household paying by Direct Debit. That is down 6.6% from £1,758 in January to March. (https://www.ofgem.gov.uk/information-consumers/energy-advice-households/energy-price-cap-explained)

3) Housing supply signals
GOV.UK’s experimental housing supply indicators estimate 191,300 net additional homes delivered in England since 1 April 2025, and 342,100 since the start of Parliament (9 July 2024). (https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/housing-supply-indicators-of-new-supply-england-october-to-december-2025/housing-supply-indicators-of-new-supply-england-october-to-december-2025)

4) Pensions and the boundary between state and capital
The Lords voted 217 to 113 to remove ministers’ reserve power to direct where defined contribution pension schemes invest. (https://www.international-adviser.com/house-of-lords-votes-to-scrap-government-power-to-mandate-where-pension-schemes-invest/)

I’ve posted the full daily briefing at https://britblueprint.com/briefing.

What would you change first if you had to improve the UK’s delivery capacity without passing a new Act of Parliament?

If you’ve been rationing heating, skipping meals, or building up debt just to keep the lights on, this matters.

Ofgem has set the energy price cap for April to June 2026 at £1,641 a year for a typical household paying by Direct Debit. That is down 6.6% from £1,758 in January to March. (https://www.ofgem.gov.uk/information-consumers/energy-advice-households/energy-price-cap-explained)

A lower headline number is welcome. But most people don’t experience energy bills as a neat annual figure. They feel it as a shock when the meter runs, and a standing charge that keeps ticking even when you use less. The price cap still includes daily standing charges for electricity and gas. (https://www.ofgem.gov.uk/information-consumers/energy-advice-households/energy-price-cap-explained)

The fix is not another press release. The Energy Secretary should publish a clear plan to cut standing charges and connect cheaper power faster, with dates and named owners for the work.

Share this if you want energy bills that fall because the system gets cheaper, not because families cut back.
